title: Strategic and tactical DDD split
summary: DDD should first clarify business goals, model boundaries, and reasons for change; implementation patterns come after that framing.

title: Subdomain classification
summary: Classifying capabilities as core, supporting, or generic controls how much modeling effort, custom code, and operational ownership they deserve.

title: Language and model discovery
summary: Domain experts, business language, and ubiquitous language are practical tools for turning ambiguous conversations into a model that can be tested in code.

title: Bounded context as semantic boundary
summary: A bounded context protects model meaning by defining where a language is coherent and where translation or separation is required.

title: Context map integration patterns
summary: Context maps expose relationships such as partnership, shared kernel, conformist dependency, anticorruption layer, and open host service so integration risk is explicit.

title: Simple business logic patterns
summary: Transaction script and active record are useful for simpler domains, but they should not be stretched into complex invariant-heavy models.

title: Rich domain model building blocks
summary: Entities, value objects, aggregates, repositories, and domain events work together when business behavior and invariants justify a richer model.

title: Event sourcing and event store
summary: Event sourcing makes event history central to the model and requires append-only event storage, changing audit, migration, and read-model decisions.

title: CQRS decision boundary
summary: CQRS is useful when command decisions and query needs diverge enough to justify separate models and operational complexity.

title: Event storming discovery workflow
summary: Event storming is a collaborative way to reveal events, commands, policies, aggregates, and context boundaries before committing to code structure.

title: DDD and microservice boundaries
summary: Bounded contexts can guide microservice decomposition, but service boundaries also depend on team ownership, runtime coupling, deployment cost, and operational maturity.

title: Event-driven architecture through DDD boundaries
summary: DDD helps decide event ownership and meaning, which reduces semantic coupling in event-driven integration.

title: Data mesh and domain ownership
summary: Data mesh extends domain thinking into analytical data ownership, making boundaries and language important beyond transaction systems.
